steps.title: The Holes
steps.text:
## Weak squares — defined

A **weak square** is a square that **cannot be defended by a friendly pawn**, *now or ever*. Once weak, it stays weak — pawns do not retreat. The most dangerous weak squares lie in your **own half** of the board, especially within range of the enemy.

### What this position is

A classic Italian-style middlegame. Both sides have castled, both have all minor pieces in play. Look closely at **d5** and **d4**: each of these squares is a **3rd-rank** central hole that one side has not yet secured. Whoever plants a piece there first dominates.

### How holes form

1. A **flank pawn push** (...g6, ...h6, ...b6) leaves the unmarched square behind it weak.
2. A **pawn break** advances one pawn but leaves another **backward**, with a weak square in front of it.
3. A **fianchetto bishop** is traded — the squares around it are forever weak.
4. **Doubled pawns** along a file leave the squares **between** the doubled pawns vulnerable.

### How to USE weak squares

1. **Spot them** — every time the opponent moves a pawn, ask *what square did he just abandon forever?*
2. **Bring a piece** — knights are the kings of holes; bishops thrive on holes that connect to a long diagonal; rooks claim files that lead through holes.
3. **Defend** the piece on the hole with a pawn so it cannot be cheaply traded.
4. **Combine** with the opponent's bad piece — if his bishop cannot reach the hole, your knight reigns.

### What signs in the position tell you to apply it

- The opponent has played a **flank pawn move** without preparation.
- A **half-open file** runs from your rook into a possible hole.
- The opponent has a **bad bishop** that cannot defend the hole's colour.

### Common mistakes

- **Misjudging** a square as weak when a pawn can still reach it.
- Failing to **occupy** the hole — a hole no one fills is just a square.
- **Trading the piece** on the hole for a less valuable one.

### A model line (schematic)

... {{Nd5}} — a knight on the hole, defended by a pawn, attacking everything around — this is the picture of a weak-square strategy at work.

> A weak square is a permanent address — make sure your piece moves in.

steps.title: Color Complexes
steps.text:
## Beyond squares — complexes

Deeper than any single weak square is a **colour complex**: a **set of squares of one colour** that are systematically weak. When the opponent's pawns are all on **one colour**, every square of the **opposite colour** is potentially weak.

### How a complex forms

- The opponent's pawns chain along a **single colour** (often the colour of his bishop).
- His **bishop on the same colour** can defend pawns but **never** the squares between them.
- He has **traded** the bishop of the *opposite* colour, leaving him with no diagonal defender.

### Two visual examples in prose

- **Dark-square weakness**: Black plays ...e5 and ...g6 with bishops traded. Pawns sit on c7, d6, e5, f7, g6, h7 — almost all on **light squares**. The **dark squares** (d4, e3, f4, g5, h6) are an open boulevard for White's pieces.
- **Light-square weakness around a king**: Black has played ...g6 and traded his light-square bishop. The squares **f5, h5, e6, g4** are all weak. White lifts a knight to **e6** or pushes **h4-h5** and the king is doomed.

### How to USE colour complexes

1. **Trade** the opponent's bishop **of the weak colour** if you can — without it, he has no diagonal defender.
2. **Place pieces and pawns** on the **strong colour** (yours) and target the squares of the weak colour.
3. **Push pawns** that fix the opponent's structure on one colour — sacrifices to lock the chain are often correct.
4. **Use a queen-bishop battery** along the weak diagonal — devastating with a colour-weak king.

### What signs tell you a complex is forming

- The opponent has **only one bishop** and his pawns are on the **same colour**.
- A **fianchettoed bishop** has just been traded — the squares around the king (one colour) are weak.
- An **endgame is heading** for opposite-coloured bishops with pawns fixed on one colour.

### Common mistakes

- **Trading your own bishop** of the weak colour — you handed away the antidote.
- **Pushing pawns onto your own bishop's colour** — your bishop is now bad and your colour is now weak.
- Treating a single weak square as a *complex*. A complex is **systemic**.

> Squares are tactical. Colours are strategic.
